Title: Electromagnetic Theory
Description: Microscopic form of Maxwell's equations, derivation and solution of the wave equation, Lorentz covariant formulation, motion of charges in given fields, propagation and diffraction, radiation by given sources, coupled motion of sources and fields, the electromagnetic field in dense media.

Description_HTML: Fundamentals of applied geometric and wave optics theory, reinforced through homework assignments, and explored in experiments conducted with lasers and optical components. Sequence with PHYS 627, PHYS 628.

Title: Optical Materials and Devices

Description: Approximation methods, scattering. Sequence.

Subject: PHYS
Title: Particle Physics I
Description: Theory, phenomenology, and experimental basis of the standard model of particle physics: fundamentals; symmetries; quantum electrodynamics; R; quarks and leptons; chirality; flavor symmetry; mesons; baryons; form factors; deep inelastic scattering. Sequence.
Description_HTML: Theory, phenomenology, and experimental basis of the standard model of particle physics: fundamentals; symmetries; quantum electrodynamics; R; quarks and leptons; chirality; flavor symmetry; mesons; baryons; form factors; deep inelastic scattering. Sequence.

Subject: PHYS
Title: Particle Physics II
Description: Theory, phenomenology, and experimental basis of the standard model of particle physics: quantum chromodynamics; parton distribution functions; hadron-hadron collisions; particle interactions in matter; collider detectors; experimental methodologies to analyze data; statistical thresholds and significance. Sequence with PHYS 661, PHYS 663.
Description_HTML:
Theory, phenomenology, and experimental basis of the standard model of particle physics: quantum chromodynamics; parton distribution functions; hadron-hadron collisions; particle interactions in matter; collider detectors; experimental methodologies to analyze data; statistical thresholds and significance. Sequence with PHYS 661, PHYS 663.
Requisites: Prereq: PHYS 661.

Subject: PHYS
Title: Particle Physics III
Description: Theory, phenomenology, and experimental basis of the standard model of particle physics: electroweak symmetry breaking; CKM mixing; Higgs couplings; early universe cosmology; Friedmann expansion; entropy; freeze-out; impact of neutrinos on cosmology; dark matter evidence and candidates. Sequence with PHYS 661, PHYS 662.

Requisites: Prereq: PHYS 662.

Subject: PHYS
Title: Quantum Field Theory
Description: Canonical quantization, path integral formulation of quantum field theory, Feynman rules for perturbation theory, quantum electrodynamics, renormalization, gauge theory of the strong and electroweak interactions. Sequence with PHYS 665, PHYS 666.
Description_HTML: Canonical quantization, path integral formulation of quantum field theory, Feynman rules for perturbation theory, quantum electrodynamics, renormalization, gauge theory of the strong and electroweak interactions. Sequence with PHYS 665, PHYS 666.

Subject: PHYS
Title: Quantum Field Theory II
Description: The purpose of this course is to apply the methodology established in QFT I to theories of charged fermions coupled to a photon. Then we will begin to explore QFT beyond leading order. Sequence with PHYSS 664, PHYS 666.
Description_HTML:
The purpose of this course is to apply the methodology established in QFT I to theories of charged fermions coupled to a photon. Then we will begin to explore QFT beyond leading order. Sequence with PHYSS 664, PHYS 666.
Requisites: Prereq: PHYS 664.

Subject: PHYS
Title: Quantum Field Theory III
Description: The purpose of this course is to understand QFT at loop level, and to extend the formalism to non-Abelian gauge bosons. In addition, we will cover a variety of special topics. This course is designed to be the last quarter of a full year sequence. Sequence with PHYS 664, PHYS 665.

Title: Solid State Physics
Description: Crystallography; thermal, electrical, optical, and magnetic properties of solids; band theory; metals, semiconductors, and insulators; defects in solids. Sequence.

Title: Solid State Physics

Subject: PHYS
Title: Theory of Condensed Matter
Description: Advanced topics include quantum and statistical description of many-particle systems, electronic structure, elementary excitations in solids and fluids, critical phenomena, statics and dynamics of soft condensed matter. Topics and emphasis vary.

Subject: PHYS
Title: Semiconductor Device Physics
Description: Introduction to the theory behind semiconductors. Elementary theory of inorganic solids; electronic structures and transport properties. Basic theory of devices including diodes, transistors, mosfets, and optoelectronic devices. Offered only in summer. Sequence with PHYS 678M, PHYS 679M. Multilisted with CH 677M.

Description: Introduction to the techniques required to make semiconductors and test their properties. Solid-state and surface chemistry of inorganic semiconductors as it pertains to microelectronic devices. Offered only in summer. Multilisted with CH 678M. Sequence with PHYS 677M, PHYS 679M.
Description_HTML:
Introduction to the techniques required to make semiconductors and test their properties. Solid-state and surface chemistry of inorganic semiconductors as it pertains to microelectronic devices. Offered only in summer. Multilisted with CH 678M. Sequence with PHYS 677M, PHYS 679M.

Subject: PHYS
Title: Cryogenic and Quantum Measurements
Description: This course introduces cryogenic and quantum measurements, emphasizing ultra-low-temperature physics and superconducting qubit characterization. Students gain hands-on experience with dilution cryostats, learning their operation, control, and measurement hardware. Through practical lab sessions, they conduct experiments on superconducting qubits, bridging theory with real-world quantum computing applications. Ideal for those in quantum technologies, condensed matter physics, or cryogenic engineering. Concurrent registration in PHYS 682 and PHYS 691 required.
Requisites: Prereq: PHYS 533, PHYS 595. Coreq: PHYS 682, PHYS 691.
Description_HTML:

Subject: PHYS
Title: Optical Quantum Lab
Description: This hands-on laboratory course trains students in optical physics and experimental techniques central to quantum information science. Covering optical alignment, fiber optics, and acousto-optic modulation, students build a confocal microscope to image single spin qubits, such as atomic defects in diamond. They implement quantum state preparation, optical spin readout, single-qubit gates, and antibunching measurements. Advanced topics include Ramsey interferometry to study qubit dynamics. The course equips students with practical skills for careers in quantum engineering, photonics, and computing, providing a strong foundation for research or industry roles in the rapidly advancing quantum technology sector.
Requisites: Prereq: PHYS 533, PHYS 595. Coreq: PHYS 681, PHYS 691.

Subject: PHYS
Title: Industry Projects in Quantum and Nanotechnology
Description: This hands-on course trains students in designing, building, and testing integrated quantum and nanotechnology devices. Working in teams, students take projects from concept to completion, developing plug-and-play systems through design and fabrication. Projects involve nanofabricating mechanical, photonic, electronic, or superconducting micro- and nanodevices using advanced tools like e-beam lithography, scanning electron microscopy, and atomic force microscopy. Students gain skills in technical communication, design, fabrication, measurement, simulation, analysis, and industry collaboration. By course end, they will have built a functional system, developed industry-relevant expertise, and gained experience in teamwork, innovation, and advanced nanofabrication techniques.
Requisites: Prereq: PHYS 533, PHYS 595. Coreq: PHYS 681, PHYS 682.

Subject: PPPM
Title: Global Sustainable Cities and Landscapes
Description: The course focuses on Sustainable Cities and Landscapes (SCL), a key approach to achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)—internationally recognized benchmarks for advancing global sustainability. It explores urbanization, with a particular emphasis on the Non-US Pacific Rim, and examines practices for creating sustainable cities worldwide. Topics include addressing the impacts of climate change on vulnerable communities, transforming water and energy systems, promoting biodiversity, enhancing placemaking, and fostering rural-urban co-development. Through hybrid teaching and methods such as case studies and peer learning, the course builds competencies in systems thinking, strategic action, collaboration, and integrated problem-solving, equipping students to critically evaluate sustainability.
Additional_Information: Social Science Area Cultural Literacy: Global Perspectives

Subject: PS
Title: Marxism and Radical Thought
Description: Surveys utopian socialist thought, anarchism, Marxism, and Leninism. Central themes include the nature of radical theory, the role of the state, human nature and the new society.
Description_HTML: Surveys utopian socialist thought, anarchism, Marxism, and Leninism. Central themes include the nature of radical theory, the role of the state, human nature and the new society.

Subject: PS
Title: Causes and Prevention of War
Description: Surveys theories of causes of war; focuses on major theories of prevention; case studies from World War I, World War II, and other wars.
Description_HTML: Surveys theories of causes of war; focuses on major theories of prevention; case studies from World War I, World War II, and other wars.

Subject: PS
Title: Methods for Politics and Policy Analysis I
Description: Introduction to quantitative analysis, concepts and methods of empirical research, applied statistical data analysis in political science. Methods include descriptive statistics, bivariate correlation, and regression techniques.
Description_HTML: Introduction to quantitative analysis, concepts and methods of empirical research, applied statistical data analysis in political science. Methods include descriptive statistics, bivariate correlation, and regression techniques.

Subject: PS
Title: Methods for Politics and Policy Analysis II
Description: Survey of multivariate model building for political analysis. Multiple regression, discrete-variable techniques, recursive systems, and cross-level analysis. Application of these techniques to concrete political problems.
Description_HTML: Survey of multivariate model building for political analysis. Multiple regression, discrete-variable techniques, recursive systems, and cross-level analysis. Application of these techniques to concrete political problems.

Subject: PS
Title: Racial Politics in the United States
Description: Considers how race has interacted with political development in the U.S. from the New Deal to the present.
Description_HTML: Considers how race has interacted with political development in the U.S. from the New Deal to the present.

Subject: PS
Title: Theories of International Politics
Description: Competing theories of international relations and strategies for testing the theories.
Description_HTML: Competing theories of international relations and strategies for testing the theories.

Subject: PS
Title: Feminist Political Theories
Description: Examines the relationship between feminism, gender, and the state. Offered alternate years.
Description_HTML: Examines the relationship between feminism, gender, and the state. Offered alternate years.

Subject: PS
Title: Political Economy of East Asia
Description: Examines the political economy of East Asia, with a focus on states, markets, and social classes during economic transformation. Offered alternate years.
Description_HTML: Examines the political economy of East Asia, with a focus on states, markets, and social classes during economic transformation. Offered alternate years.

Subject: PS
Title: Civil Rights in Post-Warren Era
Description: Analyzes development in civil rights advocacy in the United States since the heyday of the Warren Court. Focuses primarily on developments in race, gender, disability, and sexuality. Offered alternate years.
Description_HTML: Analyzes development in civil rights advocacy in the United States since the heyday of the Warren Court. Focuses primarily on developments in race, gender, disability, and sexuality. Offered alternate years.

Subject: PS
Title: The United States Presidency
Description: An ambivalent view of the presidency as the key institution in the United States political system: source of great good but also of great harm.
Description_HTML: An ambivalent view of the presidency as the key institution in the United States political system: source of great good but also of great harm.

Subject: PS
Title: Constitutional Law
Description: Surveys how the U.S. Constitution works as a structure for government. Addresses how the federal courts interact within the U.S. system of government.
Description_HTML: Surveys how the U.S. Constitution works as a structure for government. Addresses how the federal courts interact within the U.S. system of government.

Subject: PS
Title: Matters of Life and Death
Description: Examines the right to life from political, legal, and philosophical perspectives. Considers abortion, capital punishment, assisted suicide, just-war theory, and animal rights.
Description_HTML: Examines the right to life from political, legal, and philosophical perspectives. Considers abortion, capital punishment, assisted suicide, just-war theory, and animal rights.

Subject: PS
Title: Politics of the European Union
Description: Surveys the historical development and current workings of the European Union’s major institutions and policies. Offered alternate years.
Description_HTML: Surveys the historical development and current workings of the European Union’s major institutions and policies. Offered alternate years.

Subject: PS
Title: International Environmental Politics
Description: How nations solve international environmental problems. Explores major problems, processes, and current debates. Evaluates existing treaties through case studies.
Description_HTML: How nations solve international environmental problems. Explores major problems, processes, and current debates. Evaluates existing treaties through case studies.

Subject: PS
Title: Globalization and Development
Description: What is globalization? What is development? Do forces of globalization impede or enable development? How do the changing rules and institutions of globalization affect efforts by developing countries to grow their economies? This course is designed to help students think through these questions systematically.
Description_HTML: What is globalization? What is development? Do forces of globalization impede or enable development? How do the changing rules and institutions of globalization affect efforts by developing countries to grow their economies? This course is designed to help students think through these questions systematically.

Subject: PS
Title: U.S. Interventions in Developing Nations
Description: Examines theories of intervention: security, economic imperialism, humanitarian intervention, spreading democracy, domestic politics; over thirty-seven U.S. interventions since 1898 are surveyed.
Description_HTML: Examines theories of intervention: security, economic imperialism, humanitarian intervention, spreading democracy, domestic politics; over thirty-seven U.S. interventions since 1898 are surveyed.

Subject: PS
Title: Introduction to Rational Choice I
Description: Introduces the paradigm of rational choice and game theory that is of special significance to politics. Myagkov.
Description_HTML: Introduces the paradigm of rational choice and game theory that is of special significance to politics. Myagkov.

Subject: PS
Title: Civil Rights and Civil Liberties
Description: Overview of the role of rights in the United States legal system. Particular emphasis on the role of freedom and equality in a federal system.
Description_HTML: Overview of the role of rights in the United States legal system. Particular emphasis on the role of freedom and equality in a federal system.

Subject: PS
Title: United States Political Economy
Description: Examines United States political-economic institutions from a comparative and historical perspective. Topics include rise and fall of mass production, labor and the law, and regional development.
Description_HTML: Examines United States political-economic institutions from a comparative and historical perspective. Topics include rise and fall of mass production, labor and the law, and regional development.

Subject: PS
Title: Qualitative Methods
Description: Survey of rationales for qualitative methods in the social sciences and the main conceptual and practical issues raised in qualitative research. Offered alternate years.
Description_HTML: Survey of rationales for qualitative methods in the social sciences and the main conceptual and practical issues raised in qualitative research. Offered alternate years.

Subject: PS
Title: Interpretive Methods in Political Science
Description: This seminar trains graduate students in the philosophy, theory and practice of interpretive methods by engaging five methods: discourse analysis, genealogy, political ethnography, interpretive interviewing, and decoloniality. We will take some time with each approach, devoting a week or more to theory & method
Description_HTML: This seminar trains graduate students in the philosophy, theory and practice of interpretive methods by engaging five methods: discourse analysis, genealogy, political ethnography, interpretive interviewing, and decoloniality. Subject: PS
Title: Research Design
Description: This course is designed to give students the practical skills necessary to craft a well-designed research project for any type of political science research question. In this way, it prepares students for writing a successful dissertation prospectus, as well as design a second-year paper.
Description_HTML: This course is designed to give students the practical skills necessary to craft a well-designed research project for any type of political science research question. In this way, it prepares students for writing a successful dissertation prospectus, as well as design a second-year paper.

Subject: PS
Title: Teaching and Learning in Politics and Policy
Description: This course for beginning PhD students in Political Science and the School of Planning, Public Policy, and Management provides a forum to discuss teaching as as Graduate Employee and fundamental learning skills for new graduate students.
Description_HTML: This course for beginning PhD students in Political Science and the School of Planning, Public Policy, and Management provides a forum to discuss teaching as as Graduate Employee and fundamental learning skills for new graduate students.

Subject: PS
Title: Professionalization in Politics and Policy: [Topic]
Description: This course for graduate students in Political Science and PhD students the School of Planning, Public Policy, and Management develops skills necessary for future academic professionals. The specific skills taught are indicated by the course subtitle.
Repeatable: Repeatable 3 times for a maximum of 4 credits
Description_HTML:
This course for graduate students in Political Science and PhD students the School of Planning, Public Policy, and Management develops skills necessary for future academic professionals. The specific skills taught are indicated by the course subtitle.

Subject: PS
Title: American Political Institutions
Description: This is one of three required core seminars for PhD students who plan to take a comprehensive examination in American politics. We explore how political science approaches the study of institutional contributions to political and policy developments.
Description_HTML: This is one of three required core seminars for PhD students who plan to take a comprehensive examination in American politics. We explore how political science approaches the study of institutional contributions to political and policy developments.

Subject: PS
Title: United States Political Culture
Description: In this course we examine the different ways that scholars study the relationship between politics and culture, and then focus on the United States as our case.
Description_HTML: In this course we examine the different ways that scholars study the relationship between politics and culture, and then focus on the United States as our case.

Subject: PS
Title: State of the Discipline
Description: Introduction to trends in the political science profession and to the faculty at the University of Oregon.
Description_HTML: Introduction to trends in the political science profession and to the faculty at the University of Oregon.

Subject: PS
Title: Dissertation Prospectus
Description: This course is required for third-year Political Science doctoral students and will help them develop a detailed plan (prospectus) for researching and writing an acceptable dissertation in order to satisfy PhD requirements.
Description_HTML: This course is required for third-year Political Science doctoral students and will help them develop a detailed plan (prospectus) for researching and writing an acceptable dissertation in order to satisfy PhD requirements.

Subject: PS
Title: Political Theory
Description: Survey of major works in the field of classical and contemporary political theory.
Description_HTML: Survey of major works in the field of classical and contemporary political theory.

Subject: PS
Title: International Relations
Description: Survey of major works in the field of international relations.
Description_HTML: Survey of major works in the field of international relations.

Subject: PS
Title: Public Policy
Description: Survey of major works in the field of public policy.
Description_HTML: Survey of major works in the field of public policy.

Subject: PS
Title: International Political Economy
Description: One of two required seminars for PhD students planning to take a comprehensive examination in international relations. Explores how politics and economics interact in shaping economic relations among states.
Description_HTML: One of two required seminars for PhD students planning to take a comprehensive examination in international relations. Explores how politics and economics interact in shaping economic relations among states.

Subject: PS
Title: States and State-Society Relations
Description: Examines how scholars have studied the state and its relationship to society. Students develop a comparative perspective of contentious politics ranging from peasant rebellions to revolutions to transnational social movements.
Description_HTML: Examines how scholars have studied the state and its relationship to society. Students develop a comparative perspective of contentious politics ranging from peasant rebellions to revolutions to transnational social movements.

Subject: PS
Title: Comparative Political Economy
Description: Doctoral-level survey of how political factors explain variations in national or regional trajectories of economic development, including industrialization, wealth, growth, and inequality.
Description_HTML: Doctoral-level survey of how political factors explain variations in national or regional trajectories of economic development, including industrialization, wealth, growth, and inequality.

Subject: PS
Title: States and Regimes
Description: Introduction to the major issues, theories, concepts, and arguments about states and regimes in comparative politics. Offered alternate years.
Description_HTML: Introduction to the major issues, theories, concepts, and arguments about states and regimes in comparative politics. Offered alternate years.

Title: Introduction to Psychology I
Description: Introduction to the science and application of psychology. Emphasis will be placed on psychological concepts, theories, and principles related to: Research Methods, Behavioral Neuroscience, Consciousness, Sensation/Perception, Learning, Memory, Thinking and Intelligence, and related topics.
Additional_Information: Science Area

Title: Introduction to Psychology II
Description: Introduction to the science and application of psychology. Subject: PSY
Title: Scientific Thinking in Psychology
Description: Fundamentals in the empirical study of human behavior, including hypothesis formation, experiment design, behavioral data basics, and critical evaluation of scientific claims. Sequence with PSY 302, PSY 303. Students may not register for PSY 301 after completing PSY 303.
Additional_Information: Science Area
Description_HTML:
Fundamentals in the empirical study of human behavior, including hypothesis formation, experiment design, behavioral data basics, and critical evaluation of scientific claims. Sequence with PSY 302, PSY 303. Students may not register for PSY 301 after completing PSY 303.

 Science Area

Subject: PSY
Title: Statistical Methods in Psychology
Description: Probability and statistics applied in psychological research. Topics include descriptive statistics, hypothesis testing, correlation, regression, and design of experiments. With laboratory. Sequence with PSY 301, PSY 303.
Requisites: Prereq: one from STAT 243Z, MATH 241, MATH 246, MATH 251Z; PSY 301; WR 121Z or HC 101H; Pre- or coreq: PSY 201Z, PSY 202Z.
Description_HTML:
Probability and statistics applied in psychological research. Topics include descriptive statistics, hypothesis testing, correlation, regression, and design of experiments. With laboratory. Sequence with PSY 301, PSY 303.

Subject: PSY
Title: Research Methods in Psychology
Description: Practical experience formulating a research question, conducting literature review, analyzing data, and communicating original research about human behavior. Sequence with PSY 301, PSY 302.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 201Z; PSY 202Z; PSY 302; one from WR 122Z, WR 123, HC 221H.

Subject: PSY
Title: Biopsychology
Description: Relationships between brain and endocrine activity and behavior. Topics include sensation, perception, sexual behavior, drug effects, eating, drinking, sleeping, dreaming, and learning.
Description_HTML:
Relationships between brain and endocrine activity and behavior. Topics include sensation, perception, sexual behavior, drug effects, eating, drinking, sleeping, dreaming, and learning.

Subject: PSY
Title: Cognition
Description: Major topics addressed in this class include perception, attention, memory, language, reasoning, and decision-making.
Description_HTML:
Major topics addressed in this class include perception, attention, memory, language, reasoning, and decision-making.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 201Z.

Subject: PSY
Title: Social Psychology
Description: Processes underlying social perception and social interaction. Topics include aggression, the self-concept, stereotyping and prejudice, conformity, persuasion, attraction, and helping.
Additional_Information: Cultural Literacy: US: Difference, Inequality, Agency

Title: Personality
Description: Theory and methods for studying human traits, including personality tests and measures. Current research in personality. Studies of age, gender, culture, and relation to emotion and motivation.
Description_HTML:
Theory and methods for studying human traits, including personality tests and measures. Current research in personality. Studies of age, gender, culture, and relation to emotion and motivation.

Title: Developmental Psychology
Description: Survey of cognitive, social-emotional, and personality development in infancy, childhood, adolescence, adulthood.
Description_HTML:
Survey of cognitive, social-emotional, and personality development in infancy, childhood, adolescence, adulthood.

Subject: PSY
Title: Psychopathology
Description: Major descriptive and theoretical approaches to etiological, developmental, and social factors in emotion and personality disorders. Includes assessment, diagnosis, treatment, and special topics.
Description_HTML:
Major descriptive and theoretical approaches to etiological, developmental, and social factors in emotion and personality disorders. Includes assessment, diagnosis, treatment, and special topics.

Subject: PSY
Title: Music and the Brain
Description: Explores the neural correlates of our perception of tonality, harmony, melody, and rhythm and how these relate to neurobiology, brain damage, and cognitive neuroscience.
Additional_Information: Science Area
Description_HTML:
Explores the neural correlates of our perception of tonality, harmony, melody, and rhythm and how these relate to neurobiology, brain damage, and cognitive neuroscience.

Role of culture in the definition and maintenance of mental health and the definition and treatment of mental illness.

Title: Psychology of Gender
Description: Critical analysis of evidence for sex differences, gender roles, and the effect of gender on traditional issues in psychology. Topics include parenthood, violence, and sexual orientation.
Additional_Information: Social Science Area Cultural Literacy: US: Difference, Inequality, Agency

Subject: PSY
Title: Psychoactive Drugs
Description: Physiological and behavioral effects of psychoactive drugs such as alcohol, opiates, barbiturates, and excitants. The psychology of use and overuse; therapies for correcting drug problems.
Description_HTML: Physiological and behavioral effects of psychoactive drugs such as alcohol, opiates, barbiturates, and excitants. The psychology of use and overuse; therapies for correcting drug problems.

Title: Applied Data Analysis
Description: Intermediate-level practical data analysis and interpretation. Topics include experimental design, analysis of variance, multiple regression, exploratory data analysis. Extensive computer use. Honors only.
Description_HTML:
Intermediate-level practical data analysis and interpretation. Topics include experimental design, analysis of variance, multiple regression, exploratory data analysis. Extensive computer use. Honors only.

Subject: PSY
Title: Psychology and Law
Description: Introduction to topics of concern to both psychology and the law. Includes eyewitness identification, legal decision-making, criminal defenses, profiling, polygraphy, and mental-health law.
Description_HTML:
Introduction to topics of concern to both psychology and the law. Includes eyewitness identification, legal decision-making, criminal defenses, profiling, polygraphy, and mental-health law.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303.

Subject: PSY
Title: Forensic Psychology
Description: This class focuses on the involvement of clinical psychologists with the legal system. The class will discuss the approaches, ethics, considerations, and measures utilized when clinical psychologists examine psycho-legal issues such as risk, custody, therapist-client confidentiality, and competency.
Description_HTML:
This class focuses on the involvement of clinical psychologists with the legal system. The class will discuss the approaches, ethics, considerations, and measures utilized when clinical psychologists examine psycho-legal issues such as risk, custody, therapist-client confidentiality, and competency.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303.

Subject: PSY
Title: Learning and Memory
Description: Processes underlying learning and memory, including evolution. Topics range from simple forms of behavior change to the acquisition, retention, forgetting, and retrieval of symbolic information.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303; one course from PSY 304, PSY 305.
Description_HTML:
Processes underlying learning and memory, including evolution. Topics range from simple forms of behavior change to the acquisition, retention, forgetting, and retrieval of symbolic information.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303; one course from PSY 304, PSY 305.

Subject: PSY
Title: Human Performance
Description: Motor and intellectual capacities; analysis of the flow of information within the nervous system; applications of performance principles to human-machine systems.
Description_HTML:
Motor and intellectual capacities; analysis of the flow of information within the nervous system; applications of performance principles to human-machine systems.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303, PSY 305.

Subject: PSY
Title: Perception
Description: Topics covered are color, size, shape, depth, distance, and movement. Examines the relationships between stimuli and perception, stimuli and the neural response, and the neural response and perception.
Description_HTML:
Topics covered are color, size, shape, depth, distance, and movement. Examines the relationships between stimuli and perception, stimuli and the neural response, and the neural response and perception.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303, PSY 304.

Subject: PSY
Title: Psychology and Evolution
Description: Evolutionary Psychology is the application of evolutionary theory to human cognition. Topics include: survival, mating, parenting, cooperation, aggression, status, and comparative cognition. We ground theory and criticism using empirical data from many levels of analysis.
Description_HTML:
Evolutionary Psychology is the application of evolutionary theory to human cognition. Topics include: survival, mating, parenting, cooperation, aggression, status, and comparative cognition. We ground theory and criticism using empirical data from many levels of analysis.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 304 or PSY 306.

Subject: PSY
Title: Brain Mechanisms of Behavior
Description: Organization of the mammalian brain. Structure and function of the neuronal systems underlying vision, perception, motivation, coordinated movement, sleep-wakefulness, learning and memory, and affective disorders.
Description_HTML:
Organization of the mammalian brain. Structure and function of the neuronal systems underlying vision, perception, motivation, coordinated movement, sleep-wakefulness, learning and memory, and affective disorders.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303, PSY 304.

Subject: PSY
Title: Cognitive Neuroscience
Description: Integrative neural mechanisms of normal and abnormal processes in systems (e.g., selective attention, language, memory, object recognition, and emotion).
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303; one from PSY 304, PSY 305.
Description_HTML:
Integrative neural mechanisms of normal and abnormal processes in systems (e.g., selective attention, language, memory, object recognition, and emotion).
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303; one from PSY 304, PSY 305.

Subject: PSY
Title: Hormones and Behavior
Description: Relationships among the brain, endocrine systems, and behavior. Developmental effects of hormones on the brain, puberty, sexuality, aggression, stress.
Description_HTML:
Relationships among the brain, endocrine systems, and behavior. Developmental effects of hormones on the brain, puberty, sexuality, aggression, stress.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303, PSY 304.

Subject: PSY
Title: Group Dynamics
Description: Topics in small-group dynamics, including decision-making, conflict, and changes over time in group structure and behavior.
Description_HTML:
Topics in small-group dynamics, including decision-making, conflict, and changes over time in group structure and behavior.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303.

Subject: PSY
Title: Decision-Making
Description: Psychological processes involved in judgment and decision-making. Normative theories of ideal behavior contrasted with descriptive analysis of actual behavior.
Description_HTML:
Psychological processes involved in judgment and decision-making. Normative theories of ideal behavior contrasted with descriptive analysis of actual behavior.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303.

Subject: PSY
Title: Cultural Psychology
Description: Examines interdependence between mind and culture in substantive domains such as social cognition, motivation, emotion, and psychopathology. Cultural pluralism, collective identities, tolerance, and diversity considered.
Description_HTML:
Examines interdependence between mind and culture in substantive domains such as social cognition, motivation, emotion, and psychopathology. Cultural pluralism, collective identities, tolerance, and diversity considered.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303.

Subject: PSY
Title: Stereotyping and Prejudice
Description: This course examines contemporary issues in stereotyping, prejudice, and discrimination, both from the perceiver's and the target's perspective. We will cover the phenomena and processes associated with one's beliefs about members of social groups (stereotypes), attitudes and evaluative responses toward group members (prejudice), and behaviors toward members of a social group based on their group membership (discrimination).
Description_HTML:
This course examines contemporary issues in stereotyping, prejudice, and discrimination, both from the perceiver's and the target's perspective. We will cover the phenomena and processes associated with one's beliefs about members of social groups (stereotypes), attitudes and evaluative responses toward group members (prejudice), and behaviors toward members of a social group based on their group membership (discrimination).
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 306.

Subject: PSY
Title: Motivation and Emotion
Description: Adaptive human behavior; considers biological processes involved in emotions, how emotions interact with cognition, and social influences.
Description_HTML:
Adaptive human behavior; considers biological processes involved in emotions, how emotions interact with cognition, and social influences.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303.

Subject: PSY
Title: Psychology of Trauma
Description: Cognitive, neuropsychological, developmental, social, and clinical approaches to understanding trauma. Includes analysis of childhood trauma, sexual assault, domestic violence, terrorism, combat, and natural disasters.
Description_HTML:
Cognitive, neuropsychological, developmental, social, and clinical approaches to understanding trauma. Includes analysis of childhood trauma, sexual assault, domestic violence, terrorism, combat, and natural disasters.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303.

Subject: PSY
Title: Intimate Relationships
Description: Adult intimacy and love relationships. Clinical-counseling approaches: assessment, couple and family therapies, and evaluation. Models of relationship functioning and the empirical study of interpersonal relationships.
Description_HTML:
Adult intimacy and love relationships. Clinical-counseling approaches: assessment, couple and family therapies, and evaluation. Models of relationship functioning and the empirical study of interpersonal relationships.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303.

Subject: PSY
Title: Cognitive Development
Description: Intellectual development in children from infancy to adolescence with a focus on early childhood. Topics covered include perception, attention, memory, reasoning, conceptual structure, social cognition.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303; one course from PSY 305, PSY 308.
Description_HTML:
Intellectual development in children from infancy to adolescence with a focus on early childhood. Topics covered include perception, attention, memory, reasoning, conceptual structure, social cognition.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303; one course from PSY 305, PSY 308.

Subject: PSY
Title: Social Development
Description: Theoretical issues and empirical studies of social-emotional development. Topics may include attachment, temperament, moral development, family interaction, self-image, aggression, and sex-role development.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303; one course from PSY 306, PSY 307, PSY 308.
Description_HTML:
Theoretical issues and empirical studies of social-emotional development. Topics may include attachment, temperament, moral development, family interaction, self-image, aggression, and sex-role development.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303; one course from PSY 306, PSY 307, PSY 308.

Subject: PSY
Title: Infancy
Description: Mechanisms and processes that underlie and promote rapid changes in physical, cognitive, and linguistic capabilities, from birth to 24 months. Covers innovative methodologies and cultural attitudes toward infants.
Description_HTML:
Mechanisms and processes that underlie and promote rapid changes in physical, cognitive, and linguistic capabilities, from birth to 24 months. Covers innovative methodologies and cultural attitudes toward infants.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303, PSY 308.

Subject: PSY
Title: Development and Psychopathology
Description: Biological and environmental factors that shape normal and abnormal development. Analysis of how family functioning affects psychopathology and resilience in children and adolescents.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303; one course from PSY 308, PSY 309.
Description_HTML:
Biological and environmental factors that shape normal and abnormal development. Analysis of how family functioning affects psychopathology and resilience in children and adolescents.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 303; one course from PSY 308, PSY 309.

Subject: PSY
Title: Applied Data Analysis
Description: Intermediate-level practical data analysis and interpretation. Topics include experimental design, analysis of variance, multiple regression, exploratory data analysis. Extensive computer use.
Description_HTML: Intermediate-level practical data analysis and interpretation. Topics include experimental design, analysis of variance, multiple regression, exploratory data analysis. Extensive computer use.

Subject: PSY
Title: Psychology and Law
Description: Introduction to topics of concern to both psychology and the law. Includes eyewitness identification, legal decision-making, criminal defenses, profiling, polygraphy, and mental-health law.
Description_HTML: Introduction to topics of concern to both psychology and the law. Includes eyewitness identification, legal decision-making, criminal defenses, profiling, polygraphy, and mental-health law.

Subject: PSY
Title: Forensic Psychology
Description: This class focuses on the involvement of clinical psychologists with the legal system. The class will discuss the approaches, ethics, considerations, and measures utilized when clinical psychologists examine psycho-legal issues such as risk, custody, therapist-client confidentiality, and competency.
Description_HTML: This class focuses on the involvement of clinical psychologists with the legal system. The class will discuss the approaches, ethics, considerations, and measures utilized when clinical psychologists examine psycho-legal issues such as risk, custody, therapist-client confidentiality, and competency.

Subject: PSY
Title: Learning and Memory
Description: Processes underlying learning and memory, including evolution. Topics range from simple forms of behavior change to the acquisition, retention, forgetting, and retrieval of symbolic information.
Description_HTML: Processes underlying learning and memory, including evolution. Topics range from simple forms of behavior change to the acquisition, retention, forgetting, and retrieval of symbolic information.

Subject: PSY
Title: Human Performance
Description: Motor and intellectual capacities; analysis of the flow of information within the nervous system; applications of performance principles to human-machine systems.
Description_HTML: Motor and intellectual capacities; analysis of the flow of information within the nervous system; applications of performance principles to human-machine systems.

Subject: PSY
Title: Perception
Description: Topics covered are color, size, shape, depth, distance, and movement. Examines the relationships between stimuli and perception, stimuli and the neural response, and the neural response and perception.
Description_HTML: Topics covered are color, size, shape, depth, distance, and movement. Examines the relationships between stimuli and perception, stimuli and the neural response, and the neural response and perception.

Subject: PSY
Title: Psychology and Evolution
Description: Evolutionary Psychology is the application of evolutionary theory to human cognition. Topics include: survival, mating, parenting, cooperation, aggression, status, and comparative cognition. We ground theory and criticism using empirical data from many levels of analysis.
Description_HTML: Evolutionary Psychology is the application of evolutionary theory to human cognition. Topics include: survival, mating, parenting, cooperation, aggression, status, and comparative cognition. We ground theory and criticism using empirical data from many levels of analysis.

Subject: PSY
Title: Brain Mechanisms of Behavior
Description: Organization of the mammalian brain. Structure and function of the neuronal systems underlying vision, perception, motivation, coordinated movement, sleep-wakefulness, learning and memory, and affective disorders.
Description_HTML: Organization of the mammalian brain. Structure and function of the neuronal systems underlying vision, perception, motivation, coordinated movement, sleep-wakefulness, learning and memory, and affective disorders.

Subject: PSY
Title: Cognitive Neuroscience
Description: Integrative neural mechanisms of normal and abnormal processes in systems (e.g., selective attention, language, memory, object recognition, and emotion).
Description_HTML: Integrative neural mechanisms of normal and abnormal processes in systems (e.g., selective attention, language, memory, object recognition, and emotion).

Subject: PSY
Title: Hormones and Behavior
Description: Relationships among the brain, endocrine systems, and behavior. Developmental effects of hormones on the brain, puberty, sexuality, aggression, stress.
Description_HTML: Relationships among the brain, endocrine systems, and behavior. Developmental effects of hormones on the brain, puberty, sexuality, aggression, stress.

Subject: PSY
Title: Group Dynamics
Description: Topics in small-group dynamics, including decision-making, conflict, and changes over time in group structure and behavior.
Description_HTML: Topics in small-group dynamics, including decision-making, conflict, and changes over time in group structure and behavior.

Subject: PSY
Title: Decision-Making
Description: Psychological processes involved in judgment and decision-making. Normative theories of ideal behavior contrasted with descriptive analysis of actual behavior.
Description_HTML: Psychological processes involved in judgment and decision-making. Normative theories of ideal behavior contrasted with descriptive analysis of actual behavior.

Subject: PSY
Title: Cultural Psychology
Description: Examines interdependence between mind and culture in substantive domains such as social cognition, motivation, emotion, and psychopathology. Cultural pluralism, collective identities, tolerance, and diversity considered.
Description_HTML: Examines interdependence between mind and culture in substantive domains such as social cognition, motivation, emotion, and psychopathology. Cultural pluralism, collective identities, tolerance, and diversity considered.

Subject: PSY
Title: Data Analysis I
Description: Introduction to probability, hypothesis testing, and analysis of variance with applications. Includes training in the statistical analysis of data by computer. With laboratory.
Description_HTML: Introduction to probability, hypothesis testing, and analysis of variance with applications. Includes training in the statistical analysis of data by computer. With laboratory.

Subject: PSY
Title: Data Analysis II
Description: Multiple regression and advanced topics in analysis of variance. Includes training in the statistical analysis of data by computer. With laboratory.
Description_HTML:
Multiple regression and advanced topics in analysis of variance. Includes training in the statistical analysis of data by computer. With laboratory.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 611.

Subject: PSY
Title: Data Analysis III
Description: Multivariate techniques including MANOVA, factor analysis, principal components. Includes training in the statistical analysis of data by computer. With laboratory.
Description_HTML:
Multivariate techniques including MANOVA, factor analysis, principal components. Includes training in the statistical analysis of data by computer. With laboratory.
Requisites: Prereq: PSY 612.

Subject: PSY
Title: Community Needs Assessments
Description: Explores the formative steps in intervention design and implementation. Introduces key concepts and methods for needs assessments with agencies and individuals serving at-risk communities. Topics include building community partnership and using qualitative research methodology to empirically assess barriers to and facilitators of access to interventions.
Description_HTML: Explores the formative steps in intervention design and implementation. Introduces key concepts and methods for needs assessments with agencies and individuals serving at-risk communities. Topics include building community partnership and using qualitative research methodology to empirically assess barriers to and facilitators of access to interventions.

Subject: PSY
Title: Implementation with Community and Cultural Perspectives
Description: This course explores essential tools for implementing effective (or evidence-based) programs with fidelity in community settings. Topics include community outreach, diversity science, partnership and co-creation, team science, and models of change.
Description_HTML: This course explores essential tools for implementing effective (or evidence-based) programs with fidelity in community settings. Topics include community outreach, diversity science, partnership and co-creation, team science, and models of change.

Subject: PSY
Title: Substance Use and Addiction
Description: Exploration of brain mechanisms underlying motivated behaviors and dysfunctions that lead to addictive behaviors. Topics include neurobiological and psychological effects of addictive drugs, factors that contribute to addiction, societal impacts, the link between addiction and habits, and how this relates to behaviors like pathological gambling.
Description_HTML: Exploration of brain mechanisms underlying motivated behaviors and dysfunctions that lead to addictive behaviors. Topics include neurobiological and psychological effects of addictive drugs, factors that contribute to addiction, societal impacts, the link between addiction and habits, and how this relates to behaviors like pathological gambling.

Subject: PSY
Title: Intervention Science
Description: Exploration of the development and evaluation of evidence-based treatments, the field of implementation science, and culturally competent intervention approaches. Students develop clinical intervention and case conceptualization skills while learning about infant, child, adolescent, adult, and couple focused evidence-based treatments for a variety of disorders.
Description_HTML: Exploration of the development and evaluation of evidence-based treatments, the field of implementation science, and culturally competent intervention approaches. Students develop clinical intervention and case conceptualization skills while learning about infant, child, adolescent, adult, and couple focused evidence-based treatments for a variety of disorders.

Subject: PSY
Title: Clinical Psychobiology
Description: Research and theory from the neurosciences applied to clinical problems and biological therapies.
Description_HTML: Research and theory from the neurosciences applied to clinical problems and biological therapies.

Subject: PSY
Title: Methods of Program Evaluation
Description: This course provides full-spectrum coverage of program evaluation. We will cover theory, model testing, experimental design and basic statistical theory and methods. Designed to provide students with tools and techniques they can apply to program development and evaluation within their home organizations.
Description_HTML: This course provides full-spectrum coverage of program evaluation. We will cover theory, model testing, experimental design and basic statistical theory and methods. Designed to provide students with tools and techniques they can apply to program development and evaluation within their home organizations.

Subject: PSY
Title: Translational Neuroscience in Early Childhood
Description: Introduction to key concepts and methods of translational neuroscience. Uses a multidisciplinary lens to examine environmental influences on early human development, from the prenatal period through early childhood. Reviews the evidence base for interventions that aim to mitigate risk factors in these critical developmental periods.
Description_HTML: Introduction to key concepts and methods of translational neuroscience. Uses a multidisciplinary lens to examine environmental influences on early human development, from the prenatal period through early childhood. Reviews the evidence base for interventions that aim to mitigate risk factors in these critical developmental periods.

Subject: PSY
Title: Translational Neuroscience in Adolescence
Description: Examines neuroscience research on brain structure, function and neuroplasticity specific to adolescent development. Relates principles of adolescent brain development to evidence-based treatments. Topics include the effects of hormones on brain development, increased sensitivity to dopamine in reward seeking, and the neural basis of social cognition.
Description_HTML: Examines neuroscience research on brain structure, function and neuroplasticity specific to adolescent development. Relates principles of adolescent brain development to evidence-based treatments. Topics include the effects of hormones on brain development, increased sensitivity to dopamine in reward seeking, and the neural basis of social cognition.

Subject: PSY
Title: Translational Neuroscience in Adulthood
Description: Examines neuroscience research on brain development and neuroplasticity in adulthood. Emphasizes bidirectional links between neurobiology and behavior and connections to evidence based psychosocial treatment common to aging adult populations. Topics include stress neurobiology, anxiety and depression, substance use, and cognitive function over the adult lifespan.
Description_HTML: Examines neuroscience research on brain development and neuroplasticity in adulthood. Emphasizes bidirectional links between neurobiology and behavior and connections to evidence based psychosocial treatment common to aging adult populations. Topics include stress neurobiology, anxiety and depression, substance use, and cognitive function over the adult lifespan.

Subject: PSY
Title: Trauma Informed Interventions
Description: Seminar on the effects of adverse childhood experiences (ACES) and trauma on physical and mental health. Topics include stress-related psychopathology, culture- and trauma-informed clinical intervention, and socio-demographic and cultural factors related to adversity and stress.
Description_HTML: Seminar on the effects of adverse childhood experiences (ACES) and trauma on physical and mental health. Topics include stress-related psychopathology, culture- and trauma-informed clinical intervention, and socio-demographic and cultural factors related to adversity and stress.

Subject: PSY
Title: Capstone Research
Description: Faculty-supervised research credits for each quarter in which students conduct their capstone research project, which is individualized based on student interests, context and academic or career goals (minimum 6 terms; credits per term vary from 2-9 based on the term in the program; minimum 17 total credits). Project may be embedded in a community-based agency or organization. Small group advising allows for faculty-student advising on project enhanced by peer-to-peer advising/mentoring.
Repeatable: Repeatable 8 times for a maximum of 21 credits
Description_HTML:

Subject: REL
Title: Early Judaism
Description: In this course we will study the history, literature, and cultural and religious development of Judaism from the biblical period to the early rabbinic period (roughly until 500 CE). We will survey the foundational biblical heritage, with an emphasis on how Jews reinterpreted these traditions and lived among other cultures.
Additional_Information: Social Science Area Cultural Literacy: Global Perspectives

Subject: REL
Title: Introduction to the Bible I
Description: The focus of this course is on the content and organization of the Hebrew scriptures (Old Testament)--highlighting the themes, streams of tradition, literary genres, etc., in the development of its ideas, ideals, and religious expression. This course provides an introduction to the scholarly methods and research tools used in biblical studies, specifically, and in religious studies as a whole.

While the racial conversation dominates American culture, few consider the history and significance of race beyond the U.S. This course examines race from a fresh, much less examined, perspective – that of Russia, Soviet Union, and post-Soviet world, in comparison to the US.

 Arts & Letters Area
 Cultural Literacy: Global Perspectives

Subject: SOC
Title: Introduction to Sociology
Description: Introduces the central concepts, theories, and methods that define the sociological approach to investigating the social forces that shape our lives. Topics may include social structure, culture, socialization, race, class, gender, sexuality, and inequality.
Additional_Information: Social Science Area Cultural Literacy: US: Difference, Inequality, Agency
Description_HTML:
Introduces the central concepts, theories, and methods that define the sociological approach to investigating the social forces that shape our lives. Topics may include social structure, culture, socialization, race, class, gender, sexuality, and inequality.
Equivalent to: SOC 204
Additional Information:
 Social Science Area
 Cultural Literacy: US: Difference, Inequality, Agency

Subject: SOC
Title: Social Inequality
Description: Overview of social inequality, cross-culturally and within the United States. Examines relationship of social inequality based on social class, race, and gender to social change, social institutions, and self-identity.
Additional_Information: Social Science Area Cultural Literacy: US: Difference, Inequality, Agency
Description_HTML:
Overview of social inequality, cross-culturally and within the United States. Examines relationship of social inequality based on social class, race, and gender to social change, social institutions, and self-identity.
Additional Information:
 Social Science Area
 Cultural Literacy: US: Difference, Inequality, Agency

Subject: SOC
Title: Introduction to Criminology
Description: This course is designed to give you a broad introduction to criminology as a discipline, including measurements and definitions of crime, the myriad societal & personal factors that explain crime, and criminal justice system processes and responses. We’ll also consider how criminology is woven into a range of careers in research, education, government, law, and non-profit organizations.
Additional_Information: Social Science Area Cultural Literacy: US: Difference, Inequality, Agency
Description_HTML:
This course is designed to give you a broad introduction to criminology as a discipline, including measurements and definitions of crime, the myriad societal & personal factors that explain crime, and criminal justice system processes and responses.  Cultural Literacy: US: Difference, Inequality, Agency

Subject: SOC
Title: American Society
Description: Selected aspects of American culture and institutions and the ways in which they are changing.
Additional_Information: Social Science Area Cultural Literacy: US: Difference, Inequality, Agency

Selected aspects of American culture and institutions and the ways in which they are changing.

 Social Science Area

Subject: SOC
Title: Community, Environment, and Society
Description: Interrelationship of social and environmental factors in human communities, processes of community change, impact of environmental change on human communities.
Additional_Information: Social Science Area
Description_HTML:
Interrelationship of social and environmental factors in human communities, processes of community change, impact of environmental change on human communities.

 Social Science Area

Subject: SOC
Title: Social Theory
Description: Analysis of the major writers and ideas that have shaped contemporary sociology. Focus on recurrent concepts and issues that continue to challenge sociological inquiry.
Description_HTML: Analysis of the major writers and ideas that have shaped contemporary sociology. Focus on recurrent concepts and issues that continue to challenge sociological inquiry.

Subject: SOC
Title: Research Methods
Description: The development of social research; the nature of scientific inquiry and basic methods and techniques; examination of representative sociological studies from the standpoint of methodology.
Description_HTML:
The development of social research; the nature of scientific inquiry and basic methods and techniques; examination of representative sociological studies from the standpoint of methodology.
Requisites: Prereq: SOC 204Z or SOC 207.

Subject: SOC
Title: Statistical Analysis in Sociology
Description: Construction and interpretation of tables and graphs, descriptive statistics, measures of association and contingency relationships, basic ideas of probability, and elementary statistical inference applied to nonexperimental research.
Description_HTML: Construction and interpretation of tables and graphs, descriptive statistics, measures of association and contingency relationships, basic ideas of probability, and elementary statistical inference applied to nonexperimental research.

Subject: SOC
Title: Social Issues and Movements

Additional_Information: Social Science Area

Subject: SOC
Title: Sociology of the Mass Media
Description: Analysis of media events: advertisements, news broadcasts, documentaries, popular music, and television. Perspectives include content analysis, semiotics, functionalist and structuralist paradigms, and power system analysis.
Additional_Information: Social Science Area
Description_HTML:
Analysis of media events: advertisements, news broadcasts, documentaries, popular music, and television. Perspectives include content analysis, semiotics, functionalist and structuralist paradigms, and power system analysis.

Description_HTML: Categorical and cross-categorical survey of information about exceptional children and youths. Topics include history, etiology, identification, classification, legislation, alternate program delivery systems.

Subject: SPED
Title: Diversity and Special Education
Description: Provides an overview of issues pertaining to diversity in special education. Topics include race, ethnicity, culture, and disability in a sociopolitical and historical context.
Description_HTML: Provides an overview of issues pertaining to diversity in special education. Topics include race, ethnicity, culture, and disability in a sociopolitical and historical context.

Subject: SPED
Title: Disrupting the School to Prison Pipeline
Description: Grounded in critical race theory and relying on research across different aspects of schooling and human development, students in this course will engage in rich discussions around practices that empower educators to disrupt the school-to-prison pipeline and bolster protective factors for youth, families, and communities.
Description_HTML: Grounded in critical race theory and relying on research across different aspects of schooling and human development, students in this course will engage in rich discussions around practices that empower educators to disrupt the school-to-prison pipeline and bolster protective factors for youth, families, and communities.

Subject: SPED
Title: Special Education Mathematics Instruction
Description: Systematic instruction of mathematics skills for students with disabilities: assessment, planning, curriculum modification, diagnosis and remediation of persistent error patterns, evaluation.
Description_HTML: Systematic instruction of mathematics skills for students with disabilities: assessment, planning, curriculum modification, diagnosis and remediation of persistent error patterns, evaluation.

Subject: SPED
Title: Behavior and Classroom Management
Description: Provides behavior management procedures for a variety of educational environments. Emphasizes functional assessment-based behavior support planning, classroom management, and principles of applied behavior analysis.
Description_HTML: Provides behavior management procedures for a variety of educational environments. Emphasizes functional assessment-based behavior support planning, classroom management, and principles of applied behavior analysis.

Subject: SPED
Title: Special Education Law
Description: This course provides students with knowledge of standards and ethics for professional practice, historical and current case law and legislation, and application of legal principles related to special education services in school settings.
Description_HTML: This course provides students with knowledge of standards and ethics for professional practice, historical and current case law and legislation, and application of legal principles related to special education services in school settings.

Subject: SPED
Title: Introduction to Behavioral Disorders
Description: Introduces the characteristics and education of children and youth who have emotional and behavioral disorders.

Introduces the characteristics and education of children and youth who have emotional and behavioral disorders.

Subject: SPED
Title: Advanced Behavior and Classroom Management
Description: Emphasizes functional assessment-based behavior support planning, individual education plans (IEPs), and effective behavior support systems for a variety of educational environments.
Description_HTML:
Emphasizes functional assessment-based behavior support planning, individual education plans (IEPs), and effective behavior support systems for a variety of educational environments.

Subject: SPED
Title: Early Literacy for Diverse Learners
Description: Focuses on designing and evaluating instruction in the areas of reading and writing for preschool- to early elementary-aged students with disabilities. Sequence with SPED 541, SPED 542, SPED 543.
Description_HTML: Focuses on designing and evaluating instruction in the areas of reading and writing for preschool- to early elementary-aged students with disabilities. Sequence with SPED 541, SPED 542, SPED 543.

Subject: SPED
Title: Adolescent Literacy for Diverse Learners

Subject: SPED
Title: Principles of Applied Behavior Analysis
Description: The purpose of this course is to provide students with opportunities to learn and master basic behavior principles as a foundation to subsequent advanced courses. Students will master and build fluency with the basic principles of applied behavior analysis.
Description_HTML: The purpose of this course is to provide students with opportunities to learn and master basic behavior principles as a foundation to subsequent advanced courses. Students will master and build fluency with the basic principles of applied behavior analysis.

Subject: SPED
Title: Applied Behavior Analysis Assessment
Description: This course provides opportunities to build fluency with the basic principles of applied behavior analysis and the application of these principles to conduct functional behavior assessments including experimental functional analyses to determine the maintaining reinforcers for behavior and to plan for behavior change interventions. Students may enroll in either SPSY 671 or SPED 471 / SPED 571, but cannot take both for credit.

Title: Behavior Change Group Settings
Description: This course provides opportunities to build fluency with the basic principles of applied behavior analysis and the application of these principles to develop, implement, and evaluate programs of behavior change in educational settings with a focus on positive behavior interventions and supports.

Title: Assessment in Special Education
Description: This course covers current concepts and issues related to assessment in special education and includes instruction and practice in administering standardized and curriculum-based assessments. Current issues in assessment such as assessing students from diverse backgrounds and response to intervention (RTI) will be covered.
Description_HTML: This course covers current concepts and issues related to assessment in special education and includes instruction and practice in administering standardized and curriculum-based assessments. Title: History of Special Education and Disability

Title: Grant Writing

Subject: SPED
Title: Transition Planning and Instruction I
Description: Focuses on preparing youth for postsecondary education and training. Development of skills for using assessment-planning and content-instruction strategies to assist students with disabilities. Sequence with SPED 634.
Description_HTML: Focuses on preparing youth for postsecondary education and training. Development of skills for using assessment-planning and content-instruction strategies to assist students with disabilities. Sequence with SPED 634.

Subject: SPED
Title: Transition Planning and Instruction II
Description: Focuses on strategies for improving employment and independent-living skills; using individualized assessment and planning strategies, curriculum and instructional strategies, and program delivery to assist students with disabilities. Sequence with SPED 633.
Description_HTML: Focuses on strategies for improving employment and independent-living skills; using individualized assessment and planning strategies, curriculum and instructional strategies, and program delivery to assist students with disabilities. Sequence with SPED 633.

Subject: SPED
Title: Student Teaching I K-12
Description: This course provides teacher candidates opportunities to apply evidence-based practices in Special Education and reading instruction. This course meets the requirements outlined by Teacher Standards and Practices Commission for the Reading Intervention endorsement. SPED 588 is taken concurrently.
Description_HTML: This course provides teacher candidates opportunities to apply evidence-based practices in Special Education and reading instruction. This course meets the requirements outlined by Teacher Standards and Practices Commission for the Reading Intervention endorsement. SPED 588 is taken concurrently.

Subject: SPED
Title: Student Teaching II K-12
Description: This course provides teacher candidates opportunities to apply knowledge of evidence-based practices in Special Education while teaching children/adults with disabilities. Teacher candidates are expected to independently generalize and apply foundational skills practiced during Student Teaching I and incorporate new skills. SPED 588 is taken concurrently.
Description_HTML:
This course provides teacher candidates opportunities to apply knowledge of evidence-based practices in Special Education while teaching children/adults with disabilities. Teacher candidates are expected to independently generalize and apply foundational skills practiced during Student Teaching I and incorporate new skills. SPED 588 is taken concurrently.
Requisites: Prereq: SPED 653.

Subject: SPED
Title: Student Teaching III K-12
Description: Provides practical experience in teaching students with disabilities in a public-school setting under the direction of cooperating teachers and university supervisors.
Repeatable: Repeatable 7 times for a maximum of 24 credits
Description_HTML:
Provides practical experience in teaching students with disabilities in a public-school setting under the direction of cooperating teachers and university supervisors.
Repeatable 7 times for a maximum of 24 credits

Subject: SPED
Title: Student Teaching K-12 Endorsement
Description: This course provides field-based practice for licensed teachers adding a Special Education endorsement, giving them opportunities to apply knowledge of evidence-based practices in Special Education while teaching children/adults with disabilities. Students take SPED 588 concurrently.
Description_HTML: This course provides field-based practice for licensed teachers adding a Special Education endorsement, giving them opportunities to apply knowledge of evidence-based practices in Special Education while teaching children/adults with disabilities. Students take SPED 588 concurrently.

Subject: SPED
Title: Student Teaching Reading Endorsement
Description: This course provides field-based practice in reading instruction for licensed teachers adding a Reading Intervention endorsement. It provides opportunities to apply evidence-based practices in reading to design, deliver, and improve reading instruction for all students, including English learners and those with reading difficulties and dyslexia.
Description_HTML: This course provides field-based practice in reading instruction for licensed teachers adding a Reading Intervention endorsement. It provides opportunities to apply evidence-based practices in reading to design, deliver, and improve reading instruction for all students, including English learners and those with reading difficulties and dyslexia.

Subject: SPED
Title: Design of Instruction
Description: Design, development, and evaluation of instructional materials for children with disabilities. Emphasis on analysis and construction of instructional sequences for various learning tasks.
Description_HTML: Design, development, and evaluation of instructional materials for children with disabilities. Emphasis on analysis and construction of instructional sequences for various learning tasks.

Subject: SPED
Title: Philosophy of Applied Behavior Analysis
Description: Introduces students to the philosophical foundations of radical behaviorism, classic and contemporary readings in behavior analysis. Students will be prepared to understand theoretical approaches to understanding behavior, and to interpret behavior in terms of the concepts and principles of behavior analysis.
Description_HTML: Introduces students to the philosophical foundations of radical behaviorism, classic and contemporary readings in behavior analysis. Students will be prepared to understand theoretical approaches to understanding behavior, and to interpret behavior in terms of the concepts and principles of behavior analysis.

Subject: SPED
Title: Experimental Research in Applied Behavior Analysis
Description: This online graduate level course introduces students to the experimental analysis of behavior. This course will cover basic research with humans and non-human animals that explores behavior analytic principles and mechanisms of learning and behavior.
Description_HTML: This online graduate level course introduces students to the experimental analysis of behavior. This course will cover basic research with humans and non-human animals that explores behavior analytic principles and mechanisms of learning and behavior.

Subject: SPED
Title: Experimental Research Behavior Analysis Lab
Description: This online graduate level course provides students who have taken the experimental analysis of behavior course opportunities to apply knowledge and interpret the results of simulated experiments. This course will model and then have students engage in their own simulated experiments.
Description_HTML:
This online graduate level course provides students who have taken the experimental analysis of behavior course opportunities to apply knowledge and interpret the results of simulated experiments. This course will model and then have students engage in their own simulated experiments.
Requisites: Prereq: SPED 570, SPED 670, SPED 671.

Subject: SPED
Title: Ethics in Applied Behavior Analysis
Description: This course focuses on the Behavioral Analyst Certification Board Professional and Ethical Compliance Code. It provides a basic knowledge of the Ethical Code as well as different problem-solving frameworks to evaluate difficult situations that may arise during their practice.
Description_HTML:
This course focuses on the Behavioral Analyst Certification Board Professional and Ethical Compliance Code. It provides a basic knowledge of the Ethical Code as well as different problem-solving frameworks to evaluate difficult situations that may arise during their practice.
Requisites: Prereq: SPED 573.

Subject: SPED
Title: Single Case Research Design
Description: This course focuses on basic and complex single-case experimental design strategies and general procedures, as well as on issues related to clinician-researchers conducting and analyzing single-case research in applied settings.
Description_HTML: This course focuses on basic and complex single-case experimental design strategies and general procedures, as well as on issues related to clinician-researchers conducting and analyzing single-case research in applied settings.

Subject: SPED
Title: Behavior Change Procedures II
Description: This course provides students with an overview of applied behavior analysis based behavior change systems and procedures that will include designing and implementing interventions based on fundamental elements of behavior change. Emphasis is on decreasing, eliminating, and replacing behavior that is challenging to the context.
Description_HTML:
This course provides students with an overview of applied behavior analysis based behavior change systems and procedures that will include designing and implementing interventions based on fundamental elements of behavior change. Emphasis is on decreasing, eliminating, and replacing behavior that is challenging to the context.
Requisites: Prereq: SPED 570, SPED 571, SPED 572, SPED 573.

Subject: SPED
Title: Personnel Management
Description: This course focuses on the Behavioral Analyst Certification Board (BACB) Professional and Ethical Compliance Code related to personnel management. Emphasis is consultation strategies that board certified behavior analysts use to liaison with medical professionals, parents and other family members, and trainees in applied behavior analysis.
Description_HTML: This course focuses on the Behavioral Analyst Certification Board (BACB) Professional and Ethical Compliance Code related to personnel management.
